commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=7858f902c9a51e76c0f36e7afcbe80793b3fa6c0)
commands.review_summary:
Adds an opt-in deferred context-engine turn-maintenance task timeout with a timeout fence, a command-queue timeout hook, session.stalled warning backoff, config/docs updates, and regression tests.

PR surface: Source +214, Tests +635, Docs +7. Total +856 across 16 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source/proof level: current main and v2026.6.10 enqueue deferred turn maintenance without taskTimeoutMs, and the PR body provides redacted live container logs showing the configured bound and fence behavior. I did not rerun the live gateway lock-contention scenario in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] A single mechanical repair remains on the PR branch: scope the timeout-error check to the deferred-maintenance lane and cover the inner-timeout case.

target.title: fix(context-engine): bound deferred turn maintenance with a per-task timeout (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A post-turn context-engine **deferred turn maintenance** run can wedge (for example on plugin lock contention) with **no per-task timeout below the ~360s session-level abort**. While it is wedged it holds its maintenance lane, so a user message queued behind it on the same session can wait minutes before being processed, even though the model is healthy. `session.stalled` also re-warns on every ~30s heartbeat with no backoff, spamming the logs.

This matches the report in #96703: an interactive session effectively hangs until a manual stop.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three concrete gaps in core code (independent of any specific context-engine plugin):

1. **No per-task timeout was available on the maintenance enqueue.** `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance` enqueued the maintenance worker via `enqueueCommandInLane(...)` with no options. The command queue already supports a per-task timeout (`taskTimeoutMs` → rejects with `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` and releases the lane), but the maintenance lane never wired it up, so an operator hitting the wedge had no knob below the session-level abort.
2. **A timed-out maintenance worker was not fenced.** Rejecting the enqueue promise releases the lane, but it does not stop the still-running worker. A wedged worker could later call `runtimeContext.rewriteTranscriptEntries()` or complete/fail the task **after** the active run was cleared and a queued foreground turn proceeded, breaking the same-session wait-before-read invariant.
3. **`session.stalled` had no log backoff.** `session.stuck` and `session.long_running` already throttle repeats; `session.stalled` did not.

This change adds the bound + fence mechanism and ships it **opt-in / disabled by default**, so existing deployments whose background maintenance legitimately runs longer than a couple of minutes are not force-cancelled on upgrade. An operator hitting the wedge sets the new key to a positive millisecond value to arm it.

## User Impact

- **No behavior change on upgrade unless configured.** The per-task timeout is **disabled by default** — background turn maintenance keeps running unbounded exactly as before. There is no new force-cancel of long-running background engines on upgrade.
- Operators experiencing the wedge **opt in** by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` to a positive millisecond value (absent or `0` keeps it disabled). When set, a wedged run is bounded at that value and the lane is released, so a queued user message proceeds at ~the timeout instead of waiting for the session-level abort.
- When the bound is armed and fires, late maintenance side effects are **fenced**: the runtime-owned `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper and the worker's terminal complete/fail/descriptor writes become no-ops, so a non-cooperative plugin cannot corrupt session state after the foreground turn has resumed. When the bound is disabled, the fence is never armed.
- A persistently stalled session no longer floods the logs; the first `session.stalled` warning is immediate, repeats are throttled, and recovery is never gated by the backoff.

### Changes

- `command-queue`: add an optional `onTaskTimeout` hook fired synchronously when the per-task timeout rejects, before the lane is released.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: resolve an **opt-in** per-task timeout from `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` (absent / `0` / non-positive ⇒ no bound, no fence); when a positive value is set, pass `taskTimeoutMs` + `onTaskTimeout` to the deferred maintenance enqueue and trip a per-run fence on timeout (hook + defensive trip in the timeout catch); thread the fence into the worker and the runtime-context `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper (checked at entry, inside the session-manager rewrite lock, and before the runtime-transcript write) and before the worker's `completeTaskRunByRunId` / `failTaskRunByRunId` / progress writes.
- `session.stalled`: apply the existing `session.stuck`/`session.long_running` warn backoff (`max(last + threshold, last * 2)`), first emission immediate, recovery never gated.
- Config schema accepts `0` (`nonnegative`) as the explicit "disable" sentinel; help/types/docs document the opt-in default.
- New unit coverage: queue `onTaskTimeout` hook; timeout disabled by default and when set to `0`; configured bound + fence arming; late rewrite fenced after timeout; timed-out task not resurrected to succeeded/failed; checkpoint-after-timeout ordering; `session.stalled` backoff.

## Evidence

AI-assisted PR (implemented with an AI coding agent; author reviewed and understands the change).

**Static / unit validation** (this branch):
- `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:test:src` — clean
- `command-queue.test.ts` — 34 passed; `context-engine-maintenance.test.ts` — 58 passed (incl. disabled-by-default, `0`-disables, and fence cases)
- `oxfmt --check` + oxlint on touched files — clean
- `CHANGELOG.md` not touched (left for maintainers at landing)

**Real runtime reproduction (end-to-end, in a container).** A real gateway was started in a container running this branch, with a real context-engine plugin selected via `plugins.slots.contextEngine` whose `info.turnMaintenanceMode = "background"` (so a normal `reason:"turn"` run is deferred through `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance`). Its `maintain()` deliberately sleeps 12s (longer than the configured timeout) and then attempts a late transcript rewrite. The bound was **explicitly opted in** for the test: `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs = 5000`. Two real agent turns were run on one session. Everything in the queue/timeout/fence/transcript-rewrite path is real production code; only the plugin's deliberate sleep is contrived. Logs redacted (provider/model/host/ids replaced with placeholders):

```
[T+0.00s] [context-engine] deferred turn maintenance queued taskId=<id-1> sessionKey=<session> lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session>
[T+0.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 START                       # deferred maintenance begins, will sleep 12s
[T+2.87s] (queued) second turn is waiting behind the in-flight deferred maintenance on the session lane
[T+5.00s] [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session> durationMs=5000 error="CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError: ... task timed out after 5000ms"
[T+5.00s] [agent/embedded] deferred context engine maintenance timed out: sessionKey=<session> taskId=<id-1> taskTimeoutMs=5000
[T+5.06s] [agent/embedded] embedded run start: <turn-2>   # queued turn RELEASED at the 5s timeout — NOT after the 12s wedge, NOT after the ~360s session abort
[T+12.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 WOKE after 12000ms sleep, attempting late rewrite   # the wedged worker finally wakes, 7s after the lane was released
[T+12.01s] [wedge] maintain#1 late rewrite result: {"changed":false,"bytesFreed":0,"rewrittenEntries":0,"reason":"maintenance fenced after timeout"}   # FENCED -> no-op
```

Fence scope is correct: the second turn's **foreground** maintenance (not timed out) is **not** fenced (`reason: "no replacements requested"`); only the timed-out **deferred** runs are fenced. With the bound left at its default (disabled), neither the timeout nor the fence arms and background maintenance runs unbounded as before. A scripted reproduction is available on request.

Closes #96703
target.author: jerry-xin

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: feat(gateway): add OpenAI-compatible /v1/audio/speech endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a config-gated `POST /v1/audio/speech` endpoint that bridges OpenAI-compatible text-to-speech requests onto OpenClaw's already-configured TTS providers, so OpenAI SDK / Open WebUI / LobeChat style clients can synthesize speech through the gateway.
- When enabled, `GET /v1/models` additionally lists each configured-and-available speech provider as `tts/<provider>` (a separate namespace from the agent model targets), sourced from `listSpeechProviders(cfg).filter(isConfigured)` — never hardcoded.
- Reuses the existing `synthesizeSpeech` runtime and provider registry; the endpoint adds no new provider-selection, auth, or rate-limit logic (it goes through the same `handleGatewayPostJsonEndpoint` helper as `/v1/embeddings`).
- `voice` / `speed` / `response_format` are honored per request. `speed` is clamped to OpenAI's `0.25`-`4.0`; `response_format` is restricted to formats the pipeline can faithfully produce (`mp3`, `opus`, `wav`) and the response `Content-Type` is always derived from the produced bytes so it can never disagree with the payload. If a resolved provider ignores the requested `response_format` and emits a different format, the request is rejected with `400` instead of silently returning the provider default, so the parameter genuinely takes effect for every listed `tts/<provider>`.
- Out of scope: streaming audio, SSML, and per-provider format matrices beyond what the OpenAI-compatible providers already produce.

What reviewers should focus on: the new `audioSpeech` enable gate wiring (`server-runtime-config` -> `server-runtime-state` -> `server.impl` -> `server-http`), and the small `response_format`-override addition to the two OpenAI-compatible speech providers.

## Linked context

No tracking issue. This ports a feature already shipped in a sibling project to the OpenClaw gateway.

Closes #

Related #

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** new `POST /v1/audio/speech` and `tts/<provider>` listing in `/v1/models`.
- **Real environment tested:** a real gateway booted from this branch with `node --import tsx src/entry.ts gateway run` (10 plugins loaded, including the bundled `openai` speech provider). To keep the proof deterministic and key-free, the configured `openai` TTS provider's `baseUrl` points at a tiny local OpenAI-compatible `/audio/speech` mock; the gateway -> registry -> `synthesizeSpeech` -> provider code path exercised is identical to production.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
# config (isolated state dir): gateway.http.endpoints.audioSpeech.enabled=true,
# messages.tts.provider=openai with providers.openai.baseUrl -> local mock.
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/oc-proof node --import tsx src/entry.ts gateway run \
  --port 18789 --auth token --token <redacted> --bind loopback --allow-unconfigured --force

curl -sS http://localhost:18789/v1/models -H 'Authorization: Bearer <redacted>'

curl -sS http://localhost:18789/v1/audio/speech -H 'Authorization: Bearer <redacted>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"model":"tts/openai","input":"Hello from a real OpenClaw gateway.","voice":"shimmer","response_format":"mp3"}' \
  -D - -o out.mp3
```

- **Evidence after fix (copied live output):**

`GET /v1/models` (note `tts/openai` alongside the agent targets):

```json
{"object":"list","data":[
  {"id":"openclaw","object":"model",...},
  {"id":"openclaw/default","object":"model",...},
  {"id":"openclaw/main","object":"model",...},
  {"id":"tts/openai","object":"model","created":0,"owned_by":"openclaw","permission":[]}
]}
```

`POST /v1/audio/speech` responses (status / Content-Type / body), all returning raw audio:

```
mp3   -> HTTP/1.1 200 OK  Content-Type: audio/mpeg  Content-Length: 51   body: MOCK_AUDIO[mp3]:Hello from a real OpenClaw gateway.
opus  -> [HTTP 200] content-type=audio/opus bytes=28  body: MOCK_AUDIO[opus]:opus please
wav   -> [HTTP 200] content-type=audio/wav  bytes=26  body: MOCK_AUDIO[wav]:wav please
```

Upstream request log from the mock (proves the real `openai` provider forwarded the honored `response_format`/`voice` and the endpoint-clamped `speed`):

```
[mock] POST /audio/speech model=gpt-4o-mini-tts voice=shimmer response_format=mp3  speed=-  input="Hello from a real OpenClaw gateway."
[mock] POST /audio/speech model=gpt-4o-mini-tts voice=alloy   response_format=opus speed=-  input="opus please"
[mock] POST /audio/speech model=gpt-4o-mini-tts voice=alloy   response_format=wav  speed=-  input="wav please"
[mock] POST /audio/speech model=gpt-4o-mini-tts voice=echo    response_format=mp3  speed=4  input="fast"   # requested speed=9, clamped to 4
```

Validation / error paths (live output):

```
response_format=ogg     -> [HTTP 400] {"error":{"message":"Unsupported `response_format`: ogg. Supported: mp3, opus, wav.","type":"invalid_request_error"}}
missing input           -> [HTTP 400] {"error":{"message":"`input` is required and must be a string.","type":"invalid_request_error"}}
model=tts/elevenlabs    -> [HTTP 400] {"error":{"message":"TTS provider 'elevenlabs' is not available. Configured providers: tts/openai.","type":"invalid_request_error"}}
no Authorization header  -> [HTTP 401] {"error":{"message":"Unauthorized","type":"unauthorized"}}
GET (non-POST)          -> [HTTP 405]
audioSpeech disabled    -> [HTTP 404]  (endpoint not routed)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** the endpoint synthesizes audio through the real provider path, the `Content-Type` matches the produced format in every case, and the gate/validation/auth paths behave as designed.
- **What was not tested:** synthesis against the live hosted OpenAI API (no key in this environment) and non-OpenAI speech providers (elevenlabs/azure/etc.); the mock stands in for the upstream `/audio/speech` HTTP call only.
- **Proof limitations / environment constraints:** the upstream TTS HTTP server is a local mock, so the returned bytes are a fixed marker rather than real audio; everything from the gateway HTTP surface through `synthesizeSpeech` and the provider's request construction is real.

## Tests and validation

Commands run (all green):

- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `vitest run` for: `src/gateway/audio-speech-http.test.ts` (mocked unit coverage), `src/gateway/audio-speech-http.server.test.ts` (real-gateway integration: routing gate, auth, validation), `src/tts/openai-compatible-speech-provider.test.ts`, `extensions/openai/speech-provider.test.ts`, plus `models-http`/`embeddings-http`/`server-http.stages` and the config `schema.help.quality`/`schema.hints` suites.
- `pnpm test:contracts:plugins` (43 files, 941 tests passed) — the shared speech-provider surface was touched.
- `oxlint` on all changed files.

New/updated regression coverage: endpoint parsing, provider resolution (`tts/<provider>`, bare name, configured default), parameter pass-through, Content-Type selection, every validation/error path, and the new per-request `response_format` override in both OpenAI-compatible providers (honored when supported, ignored otherwise).

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior change? **Yes** — new opt-in endpoint and an additional `tts/<provider>` entry in `/v1/models` (only when the new flag is enabled).
- Config / environment / migration change? **Yes** — adds optional `gateway.http.endpoints.audioSpeech` (typed + zod-validated, default disabled). No migration.
- Security / auth / secrets / network / tool execution change? **No new surface** — auth/rate-limit/body-size reuse the shared `handleGatewayPostJsonEndpoint`; synthesis reuses existing provider HTTP (SSRF guards unchanged).
- Highest-risk area: the `response_format`-override change to the shared OpenAI-compatible speech providers. Mitigated by validating the override against each provider's supported formats (falling back to config/default rather than throwing) and by the provider unit tests + plugin contract suite.

## Review follow-up

Addressed the ClawSweeper review findings:

- **Enforce the requested format for every listed provider** (`audio-speech-http.ts`): after synthesis the produced short format is compared against the requested `response_format`; a mismatch now returns `400` rather than silently substituting the provider default. This makes the parameter take effect for providers that read a different override key (e.g. `azure-speech`/`elevenlabs` read `outputFormat`) without expanding the speech-provider plugin contract.
- **Agent-scoped TTS listing** (`models-http.ts`): `/v1/models` now resolves the TTS config against the same per-request agent context (`resolveAgentIdForRequest`) that `/v1/audio/speech` routes with, so agent-scoped `tts` overrides cannot make the two endpoints disagree.

- **Only advertise / route OpenAI-compatible providers** (`SpeechProviderPlugin`, `models-http.ts`, `audio-speech-http.ts`): the post-synthesis guard covers `response_format`, but `voice` is not observable in the output bytes, so a provider that reads a different key (`voiceId`) could still silently use its default. Added an optional `SpeechProviderPlugin.openAiSpeechCompatible` capability flag, set on the providers that honor the OpenAI `{ voice, speed, response_format }` fields (the `openai` provider and the openai-compatible factory, which all factory-based providers inherit). `/v1/models` now lists, and `/v1/audio/speech` now routes, only flagged providers; requesting a configured-but-incompatible provider returns `400` with the compatible-provider list. No hardcoded provider ids and no per-provider translation layer — the advertised set is honest by construction.

## Current review state

Ready for review. `Allow edits by maintainers` is enabled on this fork PR. Next action: maintainer review.
